name: Connectivity and digital infrastructure gaps
description: 27% of providers not using care tech, 14% of homes have no connectivity
severity_ranking: High — the 2025 DHSC adult social care provider technology survey (1,085 CQC-registered providers, February-March 2025) found 27% of providers are not using any care technologies to deliver care and support. Among micro providers, this rises to 40%. While 70% of residential providers reported appropriate internet infrastructure across all locations, 14% confirmed they did not have connectivity infrastructure to support access from all care home locations. Among large providers, one-third lacked adequate connectivity. This represents a fundamental barrier to digital transformation and AI adoption.
audience_affected: CEO/owners face the capital expenditure decision for infrastructure upgrades. Operations directors must manage the transition from paper to digital across multiple sites. Registered managers deal with the daily frustration of unreliable connectivity disrupting care recording. Frontline staff are most directly affected — poor connectivity means reverting to paper notes and duplicating work.
financial_impact: Set-up costs were cited as the primary barrier to technology adoption by 73% of providers, followed by ongoing licensing fees (70%) and staff training costs (52%). 82% of providers requested ongoing funding support and 67% needed upfront cost help. The Care Provider Alliance found 75% of providers anticipated reducing or halting digital transformation projects due to financial constraints. Infrastructure upgrades (WiFi coverage, device procurement, broadband upgrades) can cost £10,000-£50,000+ per care home depending on size and building construction. 39% of providers were unsure whether they would need faster broadband in the next 3 years, indicating a lack of digital strategy.
emotional_trigger: "We bought tablets but they don't work in half the building." "The WiFi drops out in the residents' rooms where staff actually need to record care." "We went back to paper because the system was too slow." "We can't afford the technology and the infrastructure to run it." "Our staff are digital immigrants — they find it easier to just write it down."
ai_solvability: Partially solvable. AI-powered care platforms can work offline and sync when connectivity returns (Nourish and Log My Care both offer offline modes). However, AI fundamentally requires connectivity for cloud-based processing. Solutions include: offline-first mobile apps that cache data locally, AI processing that runs on summarised/batched data rather than real-time streams, and advisory services helping providers plan infrastructure investments. The infrastructure gap itself cannot be solved by AI — it requires capital investment and potentially government subsidy.
source_evidence: DHSC 2025 Adult Social Care Provider Technology Survey (published March 2026): 27% not using care tech, 14% lacking connectivity infrastructure, 73% citing set-up costs as barrier, 70% citing licensing fees, 41% citing cyber/data security costs, 52% citing staff training. Care Provider Alliance survey (November 2024): 75% anticipated reducing digital transformation. Digital Care Hub analysis of survey findings. Digital Health reporting (March 2026): '27% of adult social care providers not using technology for care.'
government_policy_driver: Digitising Social Care programme (target: every provider fully digitised by end of Parliament), Digital Social Care Records (DSCR) initiative (80% adoption as of July 2025, up from 41% in December 2021), Project Gigabit (broadband infrastructure for rural areas), 10 Year Health Plan (digital-first NHS and social care).
competitor_exploitation: Log My Care offers a free Starter plan specifically to lower the barrier for providers with limited budgets. Nourish Care offers offline recording capability. Care Control Systems targets the 'switch to digital' decision with messaging about ease of transition. Person Centred Software's scale (6,000+ providers) gives them negotiating power on infrastructure partnerships.
content_format_fit: Digital readiness assessment quiz, infrastructure cost calculator, step-by-step digitisation guide (from paper to AI), infographic showing the digital divide in social care, case study of a small provider's digital transformation journey, video walkthrough of offline-capable care recording.
objection_link: "We can't use AI — we don't even have reliable WiFi." Counter: Modern care platforms work offline and sync when connected. Digital transformation can start with basic digital records on devices that don't need constant connectivity. The Digitising Social Care programme offers funding support for infrastructure. AI adoption is a journey, not a leap — and the first step is getting off paper.
